Saul was so poorly informed that he thought he should do everything possible against the name of Christ, believing that he would be serving God by doing so. He seemed to feel comfortable with this idea. We must not despair of renewed grace for the conversion of even the greatest sinners, nor should we despair of God’s forgiving mercy for the greatest sins. It is a clear sign of divine favor if God stops us from pursuing or carrying out sinful purposes, either through an inward working of his grace or through events in his providence. Saul saw Just One; how close the unseen world is! It is only for God to draw back the veil, and objects would be presented that are compared with which whatever is most admired on earth is insignificant and worthless. Saul submitted freely, wanting to know what the Lord Jesus wanted him to do. Christ’s revelations of himself to poor souls are humbling; they make them think very little of themselves. For three days Saul did not eat anything, and God allowed him to remain without relief during that time. His sins were now set in order before him; he was uncertain about his own spiritual state and felt deeply sorrowful for sin. When a sinner is brought to a proper understanding of their situation and behavior, they will completely rely on the mercy of the Savior, asking what he wants them to do. God will direct the humbled sinner, and though he does not often bring transgressors to joy and peace in believing without sorrows and distress of conscience, under which the soul is deeply concerned about eternal things, those who sow in tears will reap in joy.

A good work was begun in Saul when he was brought before Christ with the words, 'Lord, what do you want me to do?' And Christ never left anyone who came to that point. See the proud Pharisee, the unmerciful oppressor, the bold blasphemer, praying! And it is still so, even with the proud infidel or the abandoned sinner. What happy news this is for all who understand the nature and power of prayer, of such prayer as a humbled sinner presents for the blessings of free salvation! Now he began to pray in a different way than before; previously, he had said his prayers, now, he prayed them. Renewing grace leads people to pray; it’s just as impossible to find a living person without breath as a living Christian without prayer. Yet even prominent disciples, like Ananias, sometimes hesitate at the commands of the Lord. But it is God's glory to exceed our limited expectations and show that those are vessels of his mercy whom we tend to consider objects of his vengeance. The teaching of the Holy Spirit removes scales of ignorance and pride from understanding; then the sinner becomes a new person and tries to recommend the anointed Savior, the Son of God, to their former companions.

When we enter the way of God, we must expect trials; but the Lord knows how to deliver those who are devoted to him, and will also provide a way to escape with temptation. Although Saul’s conversion was and is proof of Christianity's truth, it could not, by itself, convert one soul that opposes the truth; for nothing can produce true faith except that power which new-creates the heart. Believers are often too suspicious of those against whom they have prejudices. The world is full of deceit, so caution is necessary, but we must exercise charity. The Lord will clarify the characters of true believers and bring them to his people, often giving them opportunities to testify to his truth before those who once showed hatred toward it. Christ now appeared to Saul and ordered him to leave Jerusalem quickly, because he must be sent to the Gentiles; see ch. . Christ's witnesses cannot be killed until they have finished their testimony. The persecutions stopped. Those who profess the gospel lived righteously and enjoyed much comfort from the Holy Spirit, in the hope and peace of the gospel, and others were won over to them. They lived on the comfort of the Holy Spirit, not only during times of trouble and affliction but also during days of rest and prosperity. Those are most likely to walk cheerfully who walk carefully.

Christians are saints, or holy people; not only the prominent ones, like Saint Peter and Saint Paul, but every sincere follower of Christ’s faith. Christ chose those whose diseases were incurable by natural means to show how desperate was the condition of fallen humanity. When we were completely helpless, as this poor man was, he sent his word to heal us. Peter does not claim to heal by any power of his own, but directs Eneas to look up to Christ for help. Let no one say that because it is Christ who works all our actions through the power of his grace, we have no work or duty to do; even though Jesus Christ makes you whole, you must get up and use the power he gives you.

Many people are full of good words but empty and unproductive in good deeds; but Tabitha was a great doer, not a great talker. Christians who do not have property to give in charity may still be able to perform acts of charity, working with their hands or walking with their feet for the benefit of others. Those whose works praise them are certainly best praised, whether others say so or not. But those who receive kindness and do not acknowledge it by showing that same kindness are ungrateful indeed. While we rely on Christ for our complete salvation, we should desire to be full of good deeds, for the honor of his name and for the benefit of his saints. People like Dorcas are useful where they live, demonstrating the excellence of the word of truth through their lives. How insignificant then are the concerns of many women who seek no distinction but outward appearance, and who waste their lives in the frivolous pursuits of dress and vanity! Power accompanied the word, and Dorcas came back to life. Thus, when dead souls are brought to spiritual life, the first sign of life is the opening of the mind's eyes. Here we see that the Lord can restore every loss; he directs every event for the good of those who trust in him and for the glory of his name.
